1: bachodesh hashlishi letset bnei Yisrael me'eretz Mitsrayim bayom hazeh ba'u midbar Sinai: 'In the third month after the children of Israel left Egypt, on that day they came to the Sinai wilderness.' The arrival at Sinai is dated precisely — the third month after the Exodus, with bayom hazeh (on this very day) marking it as a kairos moment. The journey from Egypt to Sinai is the journey from redemption to revelation: deliverance comes first, then the covenant that shapes the delivered community. Torah is given to the redeemed, not as the means of redemption.
2: vayisu miRefidim vayavou midbar Sinai vayachanu bamidbar vayichan sham Yisrael neged hahar: 'They journeyed from Rephidim and came to the wilderness of Sinai and camped in the wilderness. Israel camped there before the mountain.' The precise itinerary — from Rephidim (where water was struck from the rock) to Sinai — frames the covenant-giving between provision (water) and revelation (Torah). The camp neged hahar (opposite/facing the mountain) positions Israel as the audience of the divine theophany that will fill the mountain.
3: uMosheh alah el-haElohim vayiqra elav YHWH min-hahar lemor ko tomar leveit Yaakov vetageid livnei Yisrael: 'Moses went up to God, and YHWH called to him from the mountain, saying: Thus shall you say to the house of Jacob, and announce to the children of Israel.' The double designation — beit Yaakov (house of Jacob) and bnei Yisrael (children of Israel) — addresses the community in both its domestic (house) and national (Israel) dimensions. Moses as covenant mediator ascends to receive what he will bring down: the pattern of the prophet who meets God on behalf of the people.
4: atem re'item asher asiti leMitsrayim va'essa etchem al-kanfei nesharim vabi etchem elay (ʾattem rĕʾîtem ʾăšer ʿāśîtî lĕmiṣrāyim wāʾeśśāʾ ʾetḵem ʿal-kanpê nĕšārîm wāʾābîʾ ʾetḵem ʾēlay): 'You have seen what I did to Egypt, how I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself.' Nesher (H5404, eagle): the eagle's training flight — hovering over the nest, carrying the young on its wings (Deuteronomy 32:11) — is YHWH's self-image for the Exodus: not rescue to a destination but rescue to a person. Vabi etchem elay (I brought you to myself): the Exodus goal is not the Promised Land but YHWH himself. The land is where YHWH will dwell with his people; the destination is personal, not geographic.
5: ve'atah eem shamoa tishme'u beqoli ushmartem et-beriti vihyitem li segullah mikol-haamim (wĕʿattāh ʾim-šāmôaʿ tišmĕʿû bĕqōlî ûšĕmartem ʾet-bĕrîtî wihyitem lî sĕgullāh mikol-hāʿammîm): 'Now if you truly obey my voice and keep my covenant, you shall be my treasured possession among all peoples.' Shamoa tishme'u: infinitive absolute + imperfect — truly, truly obey. Segullah (H5459, treasured possession/personal property): one of the most precious terms in the covenant vocabulary, used only 8 times in the OT (Deuteronomy 7:6; Exodus 14:2; Exodus 26:18; Psalms 135:4; Ecclesiastes 2:8; Malachi 3:17; 1 Chronicles 29:3). In Mesopotamian contexts, segullu refers to the king's personal treasury — the most prized possessions kept close. Israel among all nations is YHWH's personal treasure. Ki-li kol-ha'aretz (for all the earth is mine): the universality of YHWH's ownership makes the particularism of election more striking, not less — he chose Israel from what is entirely his to choose from.
6: vatem tihyu li mamlekhet kohanim vegoy qadosh (wĕʾattem tihyû-lî mamleket kōhănîm wĕgôy qādôš): 'You shall be for me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' Two covenant titles. Mamlekhet kohanim (kingdom of priests): every Israelite has the priestly vocation of mediating between YHWH and the world — Israel's existence is a priestly existence of access to God and representation before God. Goy qadosh (holy nation): qadosh (H6918, holy/set apart) — the national identity is consecration, not merely ethnicity. Peter applies these exact titles to the church in 1 Peter 2:9: you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession — the Sinai covenant's description of Israel is fulfilled and extended to the church in Christ.
7: vayavo Mosheh vayiqra leziqnei ha'am vayasem lifneihem et kol-hadevarim ha'eleh: 'Moses came and called the elders of the people and placed before them all these words.' The mediatorial role: Moses receives from YHWH (Exodus 19:3-6) and presents to the elders (Exodus 19:7-8) — the chain of revelation. Vayasem lifneihem (placed before them): the covenant proposal as a laying-out, a presentation for consideration — not an imposition but an invitation requiring response. The elders as representative leaders of the tribal community carry the covenant response.
8: vaya'anu kol-ha'am yachdav vayomeru kol asher diber YHWH na'aseh (wayyaʿănû ḵol-hāʿām yaḥdāw wayyōmĕrû kol ʾăšer-dibber Yhwh naʿăśeh): 'All the people answered together and said: All that YHWH has spoken we will do.' The covenant acceptance: yachdav (together/united) — the whole community in one voice. Kol asher diber YHWH na'aseh: all that YHWH has said we will do. The commitment precedes hearing the specific commandments — a promissory note on what has not yet been fully disclosed. Moses brings back the people's words to YHWH: covenant is bilateral, requiring the community's public engagement.
9: vehineni ba eleicha be'av he'anan ba'avur yishma ha'am bedabri imach vegam becha ya'aminu le'olam: 'Behold I am coming to you in a thick cloud, so that the people may hear when I speak with you and may also believe in you forever.' The thick cloud (av he'anan) serves a double purpose: YHWH's majesty is veiled (the cloud prevents the consuming fire from destroying Israel) while his voice is audible (so the people can hear). The Sinai theophany gives both audio access (the voice) and visual distance (the cloud). Paul in 2 Corinthians 3:14 uses the Moses-veil as the image of what Christ removes for the believer.
10: vekidashtam hayom umachar vechibesu simlotam: 'Consecrate them today and tomorrow, and let them wash their garments.' Kidash (H6942, consecrate/sanctify) — the active preparation for encountering the holy. The washing of garments is external preparation that signifies internal readiness: one approaches YHWH with intentional preparation, not casually. The two-day preparation period before the third day (Exodus 19:11, 16) mirrors the preparation structures of other theophany narratives (Numbers 11:18; Joshua 3:5 — consecrate yourselves for tomorrow YHWH will do wonders).
11: vehayu nechonim layom hashlishi ki bayom hashlishi yered YHWH le'einei kol-ha'am al-Har Sinai: 'And be ready for the third day, for on the third day YHWH will descend in the sight of all the people on Mount Sinai.' The third-day descent of YHWH — the same eschatological timing that structures Hosea's resurrection promise (Hosea 6:2: after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up) and Christ's resurrection. The third day is the day of divine appearing after a period of waiting.
12: vehigbalta et-ha'am saviv lemor hishamru lachem alot bahar unegoa beqatzehu: 'And you shall set limits for the people on all sides, saying: Take care not to go up into the mountain or touch its edge.' The boundary at the holy mountain — the cordoning off of the holy space — is the spatial expression of the holy/common distinction. YHWH's presence makes the mountain holy; unauthorized approach means death. The Levitical laws of sanctuary access codify this spatial holiness into Israel's ongoing worship structure.
13: lo-tiga bo yad ki saqol yisaqel o yereh yiyareh im-behema im-ish lo yichyeh: 'No hand shall touch it; he shall be stoned or shot with arrows; whether beast or man, he shall not live.' The death-penalty for violating the boundary — the most severe possible consequence for unauthorized approach to the holy. The principle behind the boundary is not arbitrary exclusion but the protection of mortal life from what it cannot survive unaided. The incarnation is YHWH solving the access problem from the inside: the holy comes to humanity rather than requiring humanity to approach the holy.
14: vayered Mosheh min-hahar el-ha'am vayekadesh et-ha'am vayechabesu simlotam: 'Moses came down from the mountain to the people and consecrated the people, and they washed their garments.' The vertical movement of Moses — descending from the divine encounter to serve the human community — is the mediatorial pattern. Down and up, YHWH and Israel, divine and human: Moses traverses this axis continually. The washing of garments is the physical enactment of the moral-spiritual preparation for covenant encounter.
15: vayomer el-ha'am hyu nechonim lishloshet yamim al-tigashu el-isha: 'He said to the people: Be ready for the third day; do not approach a woman.' The abstention from sexual relations before the divine encounter is the preparation of total focus — not because sexuality is impure but because covenantal encounter with the holy claims the whole person's orientation. The sexual union creates its own deep intimacy; approaching the deepest covenant intimacy requires a focused reorientation.
16: vayhi vaboker hashhlishi vayhi qolot uveraqim ve'anan kaveid al-hahar veqol shofar chazaq me'od (wayhî babbōqer haššĕlîšî wayhî qōlôt ûbĕrāqîm wĕʿānān kābēd ʿal-hāhār wĕqōl šôpār ḥāzāq mĕʾōd): 'On the morning of the third day there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud on the mountain, and a very loud trumpet blast.' The classic theophany vocabulary: qolot (H6963, thunders/voices), beraqim (H1300, lightnings), anan kaveid (heavy/thick cloud), qol shofar (sound of the ram's horn). John sees the same phenomena around the throne in Revelation (Revelation 4:5; Exodus 8:5; Exodus 11:19; Exodus 16:18) — the Sinai theophany language is transported into the eschatological throne-room. Vayecheradhu kol-ha'am asher bamachane: all the people in the camp trembled.
17: vayotse Mosheh et-ha'am liqrat haElohim min-hamachane vayatyatzevu betachtit hahar: 'Moses brought out the people from the camp to meet God, and they stood at the foot of the mountain.' Liqrat haElohim (to meet God): the covenant encounter is a meeting — a personal meeting requiring both parties to be present. Israel stands at the foot of the mountain — the lowest point, at the greatest distance that still constitutes approach. The vertical distance between foot and summit expresses the ontological distance between creature and Creator that the Sinai covenant bridges through revelation.
18: vehar Sinai ashan kulo mipnei asher yared alav YHWH ba'esh (wĕhar sînay ʿāšan kullô mippĕnê ʾăšer yārad ʿālāyw Yhwh bāʾēš): 'Mount Sinai was wrapped in smoke because YHWH descended on it in fire, and the smoke went up like the smoke of a kiln, and the whole mountain trembled greatly.' Fire as YHWH's medium — his holiness consuming what it touches. The kiln-smoke image links to the Abrahamic covenant ceremony (Genesis 15:17 — a smoking fire pot and a flaming torch passed between the pieces). YHWH's fire is both destructive (to the covenant-breaker) and purifying (for the covenant-keeper).
19: vayhi qol hashofar holech vechazeq me'od Mosheh yedaber vehA'Elohim ya'anenu beqol: 'The sound of the trumpet grew louder and louder. Moses spoke, and God answered him in thunder/a voice.' The reciprocal dialogue at the mountain — Moses speaking, God answering — is the model of covenant communication. Ya'anenu beqol (answered him with a voice): YHWH's voice in response to Moses's voice. The Sinai dialogue establishes the principle of divine responsiveness: when the covenant mediator speaks, the covenant Lord answers.
20: vayered YHWH al-Har Sinai el-rosh hahar: 'YHWH descended to the top of Mount Sinai, to the summit of the mountain.' The descent of YHWH to the mountain's summit is the ultimate condescension in the OT — the infinite entering the finite, the divine touching the earthly. John 1:14 uses the same language of descent: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us — the same divine-descent pattern but now complete, personal, incarnational. The Sinai descent is the OT version of what Bethlehem fulfilled.
21: vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh red ha'ed ba'am pen-yihersu el-YHWH lir'ot: 'YHWH said to Moses: Go down and warn the people lest they break through to YHWH to look and many of them fall.' The danger of unauthorized access even from curiosity: the desire to see (lir'ot) YHWH directly is presented as potentially fatal for those not prepared. The holy requires mediation for those not able to sustain its direct weight. The veil Moses wears after Sinai (Exodus 34:33-35) is the spatial extension of this protective mediation.
22: vegam hakohanim hanegishtim el-YHWH yitkadoshu: 'Even the priests who approach YHWH must consecrate themselves lest YHWH break out against them.' Yitkadoshu (Hitpael of qadash — consecrate themselves): the reflexive is important — the priests must perform their own consecration before approaching. The divine holiness is not simply a danger to the unprepared but an active force that breaks out against unauthorized approach (yifrots YHWH). The Levitical consecration system is the institutionalized form of this protective preparation.
23: vayomer Mosheh el-YHWH lo yuchal ha'am la'alot el-Har Sinai: 'Moses said to YHWH: the people cannot go up to Mount Sinai, for you yourself commanded us, Set limits around the mountain and sanctify it.' Moses restates the boundaries YHWH established — the covenant mediator understands and enforces the access rules. The boundaries are not YHWH's hostility but his protection: he established the limits precisely so Israel could survive near the mountain. Structure enables encounter; without limits, encounter destroys.
24: vayomer elav YHWH lech-red ve'alita atah ve'Aharon imach vehakohanim veha'am al-yihersu la'alot el-YHWH: 'YHWH said to him: Go down, and come up — you and Aaron with you — but do not let the priests and the people break through to come up to YHWH.' The graduated access structure at Sinai: Moses alone to the summit, Aaron allowed partway, priests below, people at the boundary. This vertical structure of covenantal access is the prototype of the tabernacle's and temple's spatial arrangement: most holy place (high priest), holy place (priests), outer court (Israelites), outer boundary (Gentiles).
25: vayered Mosheh el-ha'am vayomer aleihem: 'So Moses went down to the people and told them.' Moses's descent to speak the divine word to the people — the final movement before the Decalogue is spoken directly by God (Exodus 20:1-17). The mediatorial descent prepares Israel for direct divine speech. When YHWH does speak directly from the mountain, the people's terror (Exodus 20:18-19) demonstrates why mediation is necessary: the people need the mediator to survive the direct divine word.
